description: Figure 5 is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrograph showing the surface morphology of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel after isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 60 min. The image displays equiaxed, fully recrystallized ferrite grains with visible grain boundaries appearing as bright intergranular networks against a darker matrix. The scale bar represents 300 μm. According to the accompanying text, this annealed condition exhibits substantial grain growth compared with the 5 min anneal at the same temperature (reported average grain sizes of ~48.3 ± 1.2 μm versus ~25.2 ± 5.0 μm), and stands in marked contrast to the 98% cold-rolled state that possessed heavily elongated, pancaked grains and shear bands oriented ~40° to the rolling direction. The micrograph thereby provides direct evidence that prolonged annealing drives recrystallization and subsequent grain coarsening in the BCC ferrite matrix, which the authors link to a significant reduction in yield strength governed by Hall-Petch grain-boundary strengthening.
